title: RPYR-0009 — `itertools.combinations`/`combinations_with_replacement`/`permutations` panic on an oversized `r`: the constructor guards `r` for negativity but not for `usize` overflow, then `to_usize().unwrap()` (itertools.rs:1205/1306/1412)
found_by: git-history-analyzer (maintainer-fixed twin #6561; tree-wide sweep proving complete population)
cpython_behavior: raises OverflowError: Python int too large to convert to C ssize_t (all three)
guarded_twin: The negative side IS already guarded in each constructor (`if r.is_negative() { return Err(vm.new_value_error("r must be non-negative")) }`) — the fix is the symmetric too-large guard. Elsewhere in stdlib every other `to_usize()` on a Python-controlled value discharges the Option safely with `?` rather than `.unwrap()` (e.g. `_io.rs:227` `self.size?.to_usize()`, `_sre.rs:651` `self.lastindex.to_usize()?`). Maintainer-fixed twin of the identical int-narrowing class: RustPython #6561 (7b36c9e) "Handle oversized __hash__ results without panicking" and #7633 (71380be) "Fix process abort on large float format precision".
notes: One root cause, three sibling constructors of the itertools module. Each accepts a Python int `r`, guards `r.is_negative()` -> ValueError, then narrows with `.to_usize().unwrap()` (combinations `r.to_usize()` :1205; combinations_with_replacement `r.to_usize()` :1306; permutations `val.to_usize()` :1412, inside the `Some(r)` arm of `match r.flatten()`). A non-negative Python int exceeding usize::MAX (e.g. 2**64) makes `to_usize()` return None, so `.unwrap()` panics and aborts the VM. Panic line numbers on the binary match the source tree exactly (no drift). The whole-tree git-history-analyzer sweep found these three are the COMPLETE population of `to_usize()/to_u32()/to_u64().unwrap()` on Python ints in stdlib — nothing else. combinations (:1205) and permutations (:1412) bodies last touched 2020-04-27 (~6-year latent). Same class as RUSTPY-0017 (_ctypes int-too-large) in the fuzzing catalog. Uniform fix: `.ok_or_else(|| vm.new_overflow_error("r too large".to_owned()))?`.
